title: The Pavement Is the Business
summary: In Hanoi, a visiting Danish urban planner and the daughter of a longtime street-food vendor disagree about a scheme to 'formalise' pavement trading into tidy designated zones — each seeing a truth the other underrates.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Ms Trang, I want to be honest about why I'm here. The city has asked me to help design vendor zones — proper bays, with water and waste collection, so your mother and others aren't constantly moved on by inspectors. From where I sit, that looks like dignity. Why does everyone I talk to seem wary of it?

body.speaker: Trang
body.text: Because you're describing my mother's stall as if it were a market stall that happens to be on a pavement. It isn't. The pavement is the business. Move her thirty metres into a tidy bay and you haven't relocated her shop — you've closed it and opened a different one that happens to sell the same noodles.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Help me see the difference. The recipe is the same, the cook is the same, the price is the same.

body.speaker: Trang
body.text: The recipe is not the product. The corner is the product. She sits where the office workers turn between the bank and the bus stop, at the exact half-minute they're deciding whether they have time to eat. A designated bay is, by definition, somewhere they were not already walking. You will have given her a better kitchen in a place no one passes. That is not an upgrade. It's a polite eviction.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: That's a fair point about footfall, and I'll take it seriously. But you have to weigh it against what the informal arrangement costs her. No clean water. No protection if a customer falls ill. A bribe to the wrong official and she's gone for a week. I'm not romanticising the bay — I'm comparing it to a situation that is also not romantic.

body.speaker: Trang
body.text: I won't pretend the bribes and the moving-on aren't real. They are. My mother has lost whole days to them. So when you offer to end that, part of me wants to say yes immediately. But I've watched three of these schemes now, and here's what actually happens. The honest vendors move into the bays. The bays are in the wrong place, so half of them fail within a year. And the spot on the good corner doesn't stay empty — it's taken by someone with a connection to the inspector, who now trades there with permission. You haven't formalised the trade. You've formalised who gets the corner.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Now that is the argument I hadn't heard, and it's the one that should worry me most. You're saying the scheme doesn't remove the informal power over the pavement — it launders it. It gives the existing corruption a clean legal surface to operate on.

body.speaker: Trang
body.text: Exactly. And the people who write the scheme never see it, because on paper everything improved. Vendors counted: up. Hygiene scores: up. Complaints from shopkeepers about clutter: down. Every number you can collect says you succeeded. The one thing you can't collect is whether the woman who fed that corner for twenty years is still feeding it, or whether she's in a clean empty bay watching her savings run out.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: So what would you have me do — recommend nothing, and leave the bribes and the lost weeks exactly as they are? Because that is also a choice, and your mother pays for it.

body.speaker: Trang
body.text: No. I'd have you fix the thing that's actually broken, which is the moving-on, not the location. Give her a registered right to the spot she already occupies. Protect where she is instead of relocating where she is. The corner doesn't need to be redesigned. It needs to be defended. You came to build something. The useful thing might be to build a rule that lets her stay still.

grammarNotes.title: Counterfactual 'would have' + reframing ('So what would you have me do…?')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cuộc tranh luận dùng cấu trúc 'would have someone do' (muốn ai đó làm gì) và đối lập 'instead of': 'So what would you have me do — recommend nothing …?' và 'Protect where she is instead of relocating where she is.' Cụm 'have someone + V nguyên thể' nghĩa là 'muốn/bắt ai làm gì', còn 'instead of + V-ing' nêu phương án thay thế. Đây là cách B2 dùng để dồn đối phương vào lựa chọn và rồi đề xuất một khung khác.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The debate uses causative 'would have someone do' and contrastive 'instead of': 'So what would you have me do — recommend nothing …?' and 'Protect where she is instead of relocating where she is.' 'Have someone + bare infinitive' means 'want/get someone to do', and 'instead of + -ing' offers the alternative. It is a B2 way to corner an opponent on a choice and then re-frame it.

translationVi:
Lars: Cô Trang, tôi muốn thành thật về lý do tôi ở đây. Thành phố nhờ tôi giúp thiết kế các khu cho người bán hàng — những ô đàng hoàng, có nước và thu gom rác, để mẹ cô và những người khác không bị thanh tra đuổi đi liên tục. Theo cách tôi nhìn, đó là phẩm giá. Vì sao ai tôi nói chuyện cũng có vẻ dè chừng nó?

Trang: Vì ông đang mô tả quầy của mẹ tôi như thể nó là một quầy chợ tình cờ nằm trên vỉa hè. Không phải vậy. Vỉa hè chính là cái cơ nghiệp. Dời bà ba mươi mét vào một cái ô gọn gàng, ông không di dời cửa hàng của bà — ông đã đóng nó lại và mở một cửa hàng khác tình cờ bán cùng món phở.

Lars: Giúp tôi thấy sự khác biệt. Công thức như nhau, người nấu như nhau, giá như nhau.

Trang: Công thức không phải là sản phẩm. Cái góc phố mới là sản phẩm. Bà ngồi đúng chỗ dân văn phòng rẽ giữa ngân hàng và bến xe buýt, đúng vào nửa phút họ đang phân vân liệu có kịp giờ ăn không. Một cái ô được chỉ định, tự bản chất, là nơi họ vốn không đi qua. Ông sẽ cho bà một cái bếp tốt hơn ở một nơi chẳng ai đi qua. Đó không phải nâng cấp. Đó là một sự đuổi đi lịch sự.

Lars: Đó là một điểm xác đáng về lượng người qua lại, và tôi sẽ xem xét nghiêm túc. Nhưng cô phải cân nó với cái giá mà cách làm phi chính thức bắt bà trả. Không nước sạch. Không bảo vệ nếu khách bị ốm. Một khoản hối lộ nhầm quan chức là bà mất chỗ cả tuần. Tôi không lãng mạn hóa cái ô — tôi đang so nó với một tình cảnh cũng chẳng lãng mạn gì.

Trang: Tôi sẽ không giả vờ rằng chuyện hối lộ và bị đuổi là không có thật. Có thật. Mẹ tôi đã mất trắng cả ngày vì chúng. Nên khi ông đề nghị chấm dứt điều đó, một phần trong tôi muốn nói đồng ý ngay. Nhưng tôi đã xem ba lần các chương trình kiểu này rồi, và đây là điều thực sự xảy ra. Những người bán trung thực dọn vào các ô. Các ô đặt sai chỗ, nên một nửa thất bại trong vòng một năm. Và cái chỗ trên góc phố đẹp không hề trống — nó bị một người có quan hệ với viên thanh tra chiếm, người giờ buôn bán ở đó với giấy phép. Ông không chính thức hóa cái nghề. Ông chính thức hóa việc ai được cái góc phố.

Lars: Đó mới là lập luận tôi chưa từng nghe, và là cái đáng làm tôi lo nhất. Cô đang nói chương trình này không loại bỏ cái quyền lực phi chính thức trên vỉa hè — nó rửa sạch quyền lực ấy. Nó cho sự tham nhũng vốn có một bề mặt pháp lý sạch sẽ để hoạt động.

Trang: Chính xác. Và những người viết ra chương trình không bao giờ thấy điều đó, vì trên giấy mọi thứ đều cải thiện. Số người bán: tăng. Điểm vệ sinh: tăng. Đơn phàn nàn của chủ cửa hàng về bừa bộn: giảm. Mọi con số ông có thể thu thập đều nói ông thành công. Cái duy nhất ông không thu thập được là liệu người phụ nữ đã nuôi cái góc ấy hai mươi năm có còn nuôi nó không, hay bà đang ở trong một cái ô sạch sẽ trống không, nhìn khoản tiết kiệm cạn dần.

Lars: Vậy cô muốn tôi làm gì — không khuyến nghị gì cả, và để chuyện hối lộ với những tuần mất chỗ y nguyên như vậy? Vì đó cũng là một lựa chọn, và mẹ cô phải trả giá cho nó.

Trang: Không. Tôi muốn ông sửa cái thực sự đang hỏng, là việc bị đuổi đi, chứ không phải vị trí. Cho bà một quyền được đăng ký với cái chỗ bà đã ngồi. Bảo vệ nơi bà đang ở thay vì di dời nơi bà đang ở. Cái góc không cần được thiết kế lại. Nó cần được bảo vệ. Ông đến để xây một thứ gì đó. Điều hữu ích có lẽ là xây một quy định cho phép bà được yên một chỗ.

questions.prompt: What is Lars proposing, and why does he think it is good?
options: Designated vendor bays with water and waste collection, so vendors aren't moved on — which he sees as dignity
To relocate vendors out of the city
questions.explanation: 'proper bays, with water and waste collection … that looks like dignity.'

questions.prompt: What does Trang mean by 'The pavement is the business'?
options: The specific location, not just the stall, is what makes the business work
The pavement belongs to the city
questions.explanation: 'Move her thirty metres into a tidy bay and you haven't relocated her shop — you've closed it.'

questions.prompt: According to Trang, what is the real 'product'?
options: The corner — the exact spot where workers decide whether to eat

questions.prompt: Why is a designated bay, 'by definition', a problem?
options: It is somewhere people were not already walking
It is too far from home
questions.explanation: 'A designated bay is, by definition, somewhere they were not already walking.'

questions.prompt: What does Trang call the relocation?
What costs of the informal arrangement does Lars point to?
options: No clean water, no protection if a customer falls ill, and bribes that can cost her a week's trade
questions.explanation: 'No clean water. No protection if a customer falls ill. A bribe to the wrong official and she's gone for a week.'

questions.prompt: Does Trang deny that the bribes and moving-on are real?
options: Yes, she says they are exaggerated
No — she says her mother has lost whole days to them
questions.explanation: 'I won't pretend the bribes and the moving-on aren't real … My mother has lost whole days to them.'

questions.prompt: What does Trang say actually happens to the good corner after such schemes?
options: It is taken by someone with a connection to the inspector, who trades there with permission
The honest vendor returns to it
questions.explanation: 'it's taken by someone with a connection to the inspector.'

questions.prompt: What does Trang say the scheme really formalises?
How does Lars restate Trang's strongest argument?
options: The scheme doesn't remove the informal power — it launders it, giving corruption a clean legal surface
questions.explanation: 'it launders it. It gives the existing corruption a clean legal surface to operate on.'

questions.prompt: Why do the scheme's authors never see the failure?
options: On paper every number improved — vendors counted, hygiene, complaints — but the one thing they can't collect is whether the original vendor survived
questions.explanation: 'on paper everything improved … The one thing you can't collect is whether the woman … is still feeding it.'

questions.prompt: What does Lars push back with near the end?
options: That Trang is wrong about the corner
That recommending nothing is also a choice, and her mother pays for it
questions.explanation: 'that is also a choice, and your mother pays for it.'

options: Fix the moving-on, not the location — give her a registered right to the spot she already occupies
questions.explanation: 'Give her a registered right to the spot she already occupies. Protect where she is instead of relocating where she is.'

options: Lars wants to help; Trang wants nothing done
Lars focuses on formal infrastructure and measurable harms; Trang on location-as-livelihood and how reform can launder power — each concedes a real point
questions.explanation: Lars concedes the footfall point; Trang concedes the bribes are real.
questions.hint: Track the concessions and the systems-vs-individual lens.

questions.prompt: Which of these does the dialogue NOT claim?
options: That the bay scheme cleanly removes corruption from the pavement
That a vendor's location can be inseparable from the business
That improved statistics can hide a real failure
That defending a vendor's existing spot may beat relocating her
questions.explanation: Trang argues the scheme 'launders' rather than removes the corruption.
questions.hint: Find the claim Trang directly contradicts in her sixth turn.

questions.prompt: The verb 'launders' (Lars, seventh turn) is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation: Like laundering money: giving corruption 'a clean legal surface to operate on'.
questions.hint: Read it with 'a clean legal surface'.

cloze.template:
Lars: Ms Trang, the city has asked me to help design vendor zones — proper {{b1}}, with water and waste collection, so your mother isn't constantly moved on by inspectors. From where I sit, that looks like dignity. Why does everyone seem {{b2}} of it?

Trang: Because you're describing my mother's stall as if it were a market stall that happens to be on a pavement. It isn't. The pavement is the {{b3}}. Move her thirty metres into a tidy bay and you've closed her shop and opened a different one.

Lars: Help me see the difference. The recipe is the same.

Trang: The recipe is not the product. The {{b4}} is the product. She sits where the office workers turn between the bank and the bus stop. A designated bay is, by {{b5}}, somewhere they were not already walking. That is not an upgrade. It's a polite {{b6}}.

Lars: That's a fair point about {{b7}}. But weigh it against what the informal arrangement costs her. A {{b8}} to the wrong official and she's gone for a week.

Trang: I won't pretend the bribes aren't real. But I've watched three of these schemes. The bays are in the wrong place, so half {{b9}} within a year. And the good corner is taken by someone with a {{b10}} to the inspector. You haven't formalised the trade. You've formalised who gets the corner.

Lars: So the scheme doesn't remove the informal power — it {{b11}} it. It gives the existing corruption a clean legal {{b12}} to operate on.

Trang: Exactly. On paper everything improved. The one thing you can't collect is whether the woman who fed that corner for twenty years is still feeding it, or watching her {{b13}} run out.

Lars: So you'd have me recommend nothing? That is also a choice, and your mother pays for it.

Trang: No. Fix the thing that's actually broken, which is the moving-on, not the location. Give her a registered {{b14}} to the spot she already occupies. The corner doesn't need to be redesigned. It needs to be {{b15}}.

blanks.explanation: 'half of them fail within a year.'
'someone with a connection to the inspector.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Trang argues that the well-meant reform would 'launder' an existing injustice — making the numbers improve while quietly displacing the very person it claimed to help. Think of a reform, policy, or app redesign you've seen that improved every visible metric while harming someone the metrics couldn't capture. How would you change what was measured so that the person Trang describes — the one watching her savings run out in a tidy empty bay — would show up in the data?
